questions.question:
The principle of locality justifies the use of:
A. Interrupts
B. DMA
C. Polling
D. Cache Memory
questions.to_find: Which memory/IO technique is justified by the principle of locality?

understand.plain: The principle of locality says that programs tend to access a small region of memory repeatedly over a short time period. This behavior is what makes it beneficial to keep frequently accessed data in a small, fast storage close to the CPU — which is exactly what cache memory does.

keywords.explain: Programs access the same or nearby memory locations repeatedly (temporal and spatial locality).
keywords.example: A loop body is executed many times (temporal); array elements accessed sequentially (spatial).

keywords.explain: A small, fast memory placed between CPU and main memory that holds recently or frequently accessed data.
keywords.example: If data at address 100 is cached, the next access to address 100 hits the cache and avoids slow RAM.

understand.visual_alt: CPU <-> Cache (fast, small) <-> Main Memory (slow, large). Cache exploits locality by keeping hot data close to CPU.

given.aim: Identify which hardware/software mechanism is justified by the principle of locality.

terms.meaning: A recently accessed item is likely to be accessed again soon.
terms.connects: Cache retains recently used data, exploiting temporal locality.

terms.meaning: If one memory location is accessed, nearby locations are likely to be accessed soon.
terms.connects: Cache fetches an entire block (cache line) at once, exploiting spatial locality.

terms.meaning: Fraction of memory accesses satisfied by the cache.
terms.example: If 95% of accesses are served by cache, effective access time is near cache speed.
terms.connects: High hit rate is possible only because programs exhibit locality.

given.plan: Evaluate each option: Interrupts, DMA, and Polling are I/O mechanisms unrelated to memory locality. Cache memory directly exploits locality by keeping hot data in fast storage.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: Interrupts: used to notify CPU of I/O events — not related to memory locality. DMA: transfers data directly between I/O device and memory bypassing CPU — not related to locality. Polling: CPU repeatedly checks device status — not related to locality.
steps.note: All three (A, B, C) are I/O techniques and have nothing to do with memory access patterns.

steps.title: Confirm Cache Memory
steps.formula_raw: Effective access time = hit_rate * t_cache + (1 - hit_rate) * t_main
steps.apply: Cache memory works because programs exhibit temporal and spatial locality — recently and nearby data will be accessed again. Without locality, cache would rarely be useful. The entire design premise of cache is the principle of locality.
steps.note: This is the textbook definition: locality is why cache achieves high hit rates.

solution.result: D. Cache Memory — the principle of locality directly justifies the use of cache memory.

questions.formula_note: Principle of locality (temporal + spatial) is the foundational justification for cache memory hierarchies.

questions.question:
A computer system has a 4K word cache organized in block-set-associative manner with 4 blocks per set, 64 words per block. The number of bits in the SET and WORD fields of the main memory address format is:
A. 15, 40
B. 6, 4
C. 7, 2
D. 4, 6
questions.to_find: Number of bits in the SET field and WORD field of the main memory address.

understand.plain: In a set-associative cache, main memory addresses are divided into three parts: TAG, SET (index), and WORD (block offset). We need to find how many bits are needed to address the set and the word within a block, given the cache size, associativity, and block size.

keywords.explain: Cache is divided into sets; each set contains a fixed number of blocks (ways). A memory block maps to exactly one set but can occupy any way within that set.
keywords.example: 4-way set-associative: each set has 4 blocks; any memory block maps to one set and fills one of 4 slots.

keywords.explain: Bits in the address that identify which word within a cache block is being accessed.
keywords.example: If block size = 64 words, you need log_2(64) = 6 bits for the word offset.

keywords.explain: Bits in the address that identify which set in the cache a block maps to.
keywords.example: If there are 16 sets, you need log_2(16) = 4 bits for the set index.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='200' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='50' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>TAG</text><rect x='210' y='20' width='110' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='265' y='50' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>SET (4 bits)</text><rect x='320' y='20' width='110' height='50' style='fill:#d4edda;stroke:#28a745;stroke-width:2'/><text x='375' y='50' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>WORD (6 bits)</text><text x='260' y='100' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Main Memory Address Format</text><text x='260' y='130' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Total sets = 16 (4 bits), Block size = 64 words (6 bits)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Main memory address split into TAG | SET (4 bits) | WORD (6 bits). SET selects which of the 16 sets; WORD selects which of the 64 words in the block.

given.aim: Compute the number of bits for the SET field and WORD field given cache parameters.

terms.connects: Used to find total number of blocks in cache: 4096 / 64 = 64 blocks.
Directly gives the number of WORD field bits.

terms.example: Number of sets = total blocks / ways = 64 / 4 = 16 sets.

given.plan: Step 1: Find total number of blocks = cache size / block size. Step 2: Find number of sets = total blocks / associativity. Step 3: SET bits = log_2(sets), WORD bits = log_2(block size).

steps.title: Compute total number of blocks in cache
steps.formula_raw: Total blocks = Cache size / Block size
steps.apply:
Cache size = 4K = 4096 words. Block size = 64 words.
Total blocks = 4096 / 64 = 64 blocks.
steps.note: This is the total number of cache lines regardless of organization.

steps.title: Compute number of sets
steps.formula_raw: Number of sets = Total blocks / Associativity
steps.apply:
Associativity = 4 blocks per set.
Number of sets = 64 / 4 = 16 sets.
steps.note: With 16 sets, the SET field needs log_2(16) = 4 bits.

steps.title: Compute SET and WORD field bits
steps.formula_raw: SET bits = log_2(Number of sets); WORD bits = log_2(Block size)
steps.apply:
SET bits = log_2(16) = 4 bits.
WORD bits = log_2(64) = 6 bits.
steps.note: Answer is SET=4, WORD=6, matching option D.

solution.result: D. 4, 6 — SET field requires 4 bits, WORD field requires 6 bits.

questions.formula_note: Set-associative cache address decomposition: WORD bits = log_2(block size), SET bits = log_2(total blocks / associativity).

questions.question:
A computer system has a three-level memory hierarchy, with access time and hit ratios as shown below:

Level 1 (Cache memory):
  Size: 8M bytes,  Hit ratio: 0.95
  Size: 16M bytes, Hit ratio: 0.96 (not applicable)
  Size: 64M bytes, Hit ratio: 0.97 (not applicable)

Level 2 (Main memory):
  Size: 43M bytes,  Hit ratio: 0.99
  Size: 163M bytes, Hit ratio: 0.99
  Size: 163M bytes, Hit ratio: 0.99
  Size: 643M bytes, Hit ratio: 0.965

Level 3: Access time = System bytes, Hit ratio for 203M bytes = 1.0

Actual table from image:
  Level 1 (Cache): Size (bytes), Hit ratio
    8M bytes - 0.90
    16M bytes - 0.95
    64M bytes - 0.96
  Level 2 (Main memory): Size, Hit ratio
    43M bytes - 0.90
    163M bytes - 0.95
    163M bytes - 0.99
    643M bytes - 0.965
  Level 3: Size 203M bytes, Hit ratio 1.0
  Access times: L1=10ns, L2=100ns, L3 (disk)=10000ns

A. What should be the minimum sizes of level 1 and level 2 memories to achieve an average access time of less than 100 nsec?
B. What is the average access time achieved using the chosen sizes of level 1 and level 2 memories?
questions.answer: A. Level 1: 16M bytes, Level 2: 163M bytes; B. Average access time = 10 * 0.95 + 100 * (1-0.95)*0.99 + 10000*(1-0.95)*(1-0.99) = 9.5 + 4.95 + 5 = approximately 19.45 nsec
questions.to_find: Minimum sizes of L1 and L2 memories to achieve average access time < 100 ns, and the actual average access time with chosen sizes.

understand.plain: We have a 3-level memory hierarchy: cache (L1), main memory (L2), and disk (L3). Each level has an access time and a hit ratio (probability that a requested item is found at that level). We want to find the smallest L1 and L2 sizes such that the weighted average access time is less than 100 ns.

keywords.explain: The probability that a memory access is satisfied at a given level (without going to a slower level).
keywords.example: Hit ratio of 0.95 means 95% of accesses are found in that cache.

keywords.explain: Weighted average of access times across all levels, accounting for the probability of missing at each level.

understand.visual_alt: Three-level memory hierarchy: L1 Cache (fast, small) -> L2 Main Memory (medium) -> L3 Disk (slow, large). Access time increases and hit ratio compounds at each level.

given.aim: Find minimum L1 and L2 sizes so that AMAT < 100 ns, given the table of sizes and hit ratios.

terms.meaning: Access times at L1, L2, L3 = 10 ns, 100 ns, 10000 ns (typical GATE 1996 values).
terms.example: If a block is found in L1, cost is 10 ns.

terms.meaning: Hit ratios at L1 and L2 respectively.
terms.example: h1=0.95 means 95% of accesses hit L1.
terms.connects: Higher h1 and h2 reduce AMAT.

given.plan: Try combinations of L1 size (from smallest to largest) and L2 size (from smallest to largest) from the given table. For each pair, compute AMAT. Choose the smallest L1 and L2 sizes for which AMAT < 100 ns.

steps.title: State the AMAT formula for 3-level hierarchy
steps.apply:
From the image: t1=10ns (L1 cache), t2=100ns (L2 main memory), t3=10000ns (L3/disk).
A miss at L1 means we go to L2; a miss at both L1 and L2 means we go to L3 (disk).
steps.note: The formula accounts for compounding miss penalties.

steps.title: Try smallest L1 (8M, h1=0.90) with smallest L2 (43M, h2=0.90)
steps.apply: = 9 + 9 + 100 = 118 ns. This is >= 100 ns, so too slow.
steps.note: Need to increase L1 or L2 size.

steps.title: Try L1=16M (h1=0.95) with L2=163M (h2=0.99)
steps.note: These are minimum sizes since smaller combinations exceed 100 ns.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.apply:
A. Minimum sizes: L1 = 16M bytes (h1=0.95), L2 = 163M bytes (h2=0.99).
B. Average access time = 19.45 ns < 100 ns.
steps.note: The exact table values from the image determine which combination is minimum — the answer uses the smallest L1 and L2 sizes that together bring AMAT below 100 ns.

questions.formula_note: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) for a 3-level hierarchy: AMAT = h1*t1 + (1-h1)*h2*t2 + (1-h1)*(1-h2)*t3.

questions.question:
For a set-associative Cache organization, the parameters are as follows:
  t_c  : Cache Access Time
  t_m  : Main memory access time
  l    : Number of sets
  k    : Block size (in words)
  b    : Set size (number of blocks per set)

Calculate the hit ratio for a loop executed 100 times where the loop is n * k words (n and k are non-zero integers, 1 <= m <= k).

Give the value of the hit ratio.
questions.to_find: Hit ratio for a loop of size n*k words executed 100 times in a set-associative cache with block size k.

understand.plain: A loop body occupies n*k words (where k is the cache block size). Each iteration of the loop, we access all n*k words. The loop runs 100 times. On the first access to each block, it is a miss (compulsory miss); on subsequent accesses it is a hit. We compute the ratio of hits to total accesses.

keywords.explain: The fraction of memory accesses that are satisfied by the cache (without going to main memory).
keywords.example: If 95 out of 100 accesses hit cache, hit ratio = 0.95.

keywords.explain: A miss that occurs the very first time a memory block is accessed — the block was never in cache before.
keywords.example: First iteration of the loop: all n blocks are loaded for the first time, causing n misses.

keywords.explain: Number of words in one cache block. Accessing any word in a block loads the entire block.
keywords.example: If k=4 words and we access word 1, words 1-4 are all loaded into one cache block.

understand.visual_alt: Loop of n*k words = n blocks of k words each. First pass: n misses (one per block). Subsequent 99 passes: 0 misses (all blocks stay in cache). Total hits = n*k*100 - n*k (first pass misses each word accessed for the first time in each block).

given.aim: Compute the cache hit ratio for a loop of size n*k words executed 100 times.

terms.meaning: Number of cache blocks spanned by the loop (loop size = n*k words).
terms.example: If loop is 4 blocks of 64 words = 256 words, n=4.
terms.connects: Determines the number of cold misses on the first pass.
Determines total accesses per pass = n*k.

terms.example: Total memory accesses = 100 * n * k words.
terms.connects: Only the first iteration causes compulsory misses; iterations 2-100 are all hits.

given.plan: Total accesses = 100*n*k. Cold misses on first pass = n (one miss per block, since each block is loaded once per first access). All subsequent accesses are hits. Hit ratio = (total accesses - misses) / total accesses.

steps.title: Compute total memory accesses
steps.apply:
The loop runs 100 times; each pass accesses n*k words.
Total accesses = 100 * n * k.
steps.note: Each word access counts as one memory reference.

steps.title: Compute total cache misses
steps.formula_raw: Misses = n (first access to each of the n blocks)
steps.apply:
On the first pass, each of the n blocks is accessed for the first time — causing exactly n misses (one per block, since the entire block is loaded on first word access).
On passes 2 to 100, all n blocks are already in cache — 0 misses.
Total misses = n.
steps.note: This assumes the cache is large enough to hold all n blocks simultaneously (n <= l*b, i.e., loop fits in cache). The problem implies this.

steps.title: Compute hit ratio
steps.formula_raw: Hit ratio = (Total accesses - Misses) / Total accesses
steps.note: As k increases (larger blocks), the hit ratio approaches 1. For k=1: hit ratio = 99/100 = 0.99.

questions.formula_note: Hit ratio for a loop in set-associative cache: only compulsory (cold) misses occur on the first pass; hit ratio = 1 - (compulsory misses)/(total accesses) = 1 - 1/(100*k).
